title: AlexNet and the ImageNet Moment
concept: In 2012, AlexNet slashed ImageNet error by a startling margin, showing that a deeper CNN trained with GPU compute and lots of labeled data could beat older vision systems on a public benchmark.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the last unit, how were LeNet, AlexNet, and VGG introduced?

options.text: As named CNN recipes built from familiar building blocks
As image file formats used to store filters
As camera brands that collect training data

exercises.explain: Right — named architectures are recognizable CNN recipes, not a brand-new subject unrelated to the blocks you already know.

cards.title: One contest result changed the room
cards.body: In 2012, a CNN called AlexNet won the ImageNet competition by such a big margin that researchers could not shrug it off as luck. Deep learning went from interesting to impossible to ignore.

cards.title: What people mean by the ImageNet moment
cards.body: The ImageNet moment usually means AlexNet's 2012 result in the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC). Its top-5 error dropped from roughly 26% in the previous best systems to about 15%, a huge leap on a hard public benchmark.

cards.title: Not a photo finish — a blowout
cards.body: Imagine a race where winning by one second is normal, then one team wins by half a lap. That is why people remember AlexNet's result: it was not just first place, it was a visible gap.

cards.title: What AlexNet was actually doing
cards.body: AlexNet took an input image and passed it through a deeper CNN stack that learned edges, textures, parts, and object clues across layers. On ImageNet's 1000 categories — things like dogs, keyboards, mushrooms, and fire trucks — it produced a ranked list of guesses much better than older pipelines.

cards.title: It was not just hype or just hardware
cards.body: People sometimes retell 2012 like a publicity event or a GPU-only story. The real lesson is architectural: a deeper learned CNN, trained at scale with the right compute, could outperform hand-crafted vision systems on a serious benchmark.

cards.title: Why the result felt different
cards.body: AlexNet mattered because the win was both public and architecture-linked.

left.text:
Older systems led ImageNet
Smaller improvements between teams
Hand-crafted features were standard

right.text:
Much lower error on ImageNet
Deeper CNN learned features from pixels
Deep vision became hard to dismiss

exercises.prompt: Why was AlexNet's 2012 win hard for the vision community to ignore?

options.text: It won a major public benchmark by a large margin with a deeper CNN
It was the first model to use images of any kind
It avoided neural networks and returned to hand-written rules
It only solved one tiny private dataset with two classes

exercises.explain: Exactly — the combination of a public benchmark, a big error drop, and a deep CNN made the result concrete and convincing.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The ImageNet moment mainly showed that a learned deep CNN could beat older vision pipelines on a large shared benchmark.
exercises.explain: True — that is the key reason the 2012 result mattered so much.

exercises.sentence: In 2012, AlexNet made deep CNNs impossible to ignore by winning the ___ competition.
exercises.explain: Correct — the famous breakthrough happened on the ImageNet benchmark.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the ImageNet moment?

options.text: A deeper CNN trained at scale beat older vision systems on a broad public benchmark
A camera company released a new sensor with more megapixels
Researchers proved object recognition no longer needed training data

exercises.explain: Right — the point was not hype alone. It was a strong benchmark win tied to a real CNN design.

exercises.prompt: A classmate says, 'Why do people talk about AlexNet's 2012 win so much? Models win contests all the time.' Which answer is best?

options.text: Because AlexNet cut error by a large margin on a hard public benchmark, showing that a deeper CNN trained with modern compute could outperform older hand-crafted vision pipelines
Because it was the only model in the contest and won automatically
Because it replaced image classification with manual labeling by humans
Because it proved small private datasets matter more than shared benchmarks

exercises.explain: Exactly. AlexNet's 2012 result was concrete, public, and architecture-linked: a deeper learned CNN won big on ImageNet. Next: why ImageNet itself was such a powerful benchmark.
